---
title: "ClinicalRWE: Oncology Real-World Evidence Study"
author: "Ludovic Njiosseu"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Executive Summary

This study evaluated whether preventive maintenance biologic therapy was associated with delayed disease progression in a longitudinal oncology real-world evidence cohort. Across survival, causal inference, missing-data, competing-risk, and subgroup analyses, preventive therapy demonstrated directionally favorable and statistically robust associations with progression-free survival.

Key findings:

- Improved progression-free survival among treated patients (log-rank p < 0.05)
- Robust hazard ratio estimates after multivariable Cox adjustment
- Negative ATE confirmed by both IPTW (with bootstrap 95% CI) and doubly robust AIPW estimator
- E-value analysis confirms robustness to unmeasured confounding
- Covariate balance achieved across all covariates after stabilized IPTW weighting (all |SMD| < 0.10)
- MICE pooled estimates stable relative to complete-case analysis
- Fine-Gray subdistribution HR differs meaningfully from cause-specific HR — treatment discontinuation is a non-negligible competing event requiring separate reporting
- Biomarker-stratified subgroup analysis and formal interaction test reported per oncology RWE standards

# Research Question

Does receipt of a preventive maintenance biologic therapy delay time to disease progression, and does this effect persist after rigorously adjusting for confounding, treatment-selection bias, missing data, competing discontinuation events, and biomarker subgroup heterogeneity?

# Why This Project Exists

Pharmaceutical real-world evidence requires a methodological toolkit that goes beyond standard ML workflows: longitudinal EHR/claims linkage, comparative effectiveness research, time-to-event modeling, competing risks methodology, causal inference under non-random treatment assignment, and regulatory-style reproducibility.

This project builds that full stack from synthetic cohort generation through interactive dashboards and reproducible reporting in the same methodological register as FDA-aligned oncology observational studies and HEOR evidence-generation pipelines.

# Methods

This project implemented a longitudinal oncology RWE workflow modeled after sponsor-side pharmacoepidemiology and HEOR analytics pipelines.

Methods:

- Kaplan-Meier survival analysis with log-rank testing
- Cox proportional hazards regression with Schoenfeld diagnostics
- Stabilized IPTW (99th-percentile trimmed) with bootstrap 95% CI
- Doubly robust AIPW estimation with analytic standard errors
- E-value sensitivity analysis (VanderWeele & Ding, 2017)
- Covariate balance via SMD computed from data
- Multiple imputation using MICE (m = 10, Rubin's Rules)
- Fine-Gray competing risks modeling via `cmprsk`
- Biomarker-stratified subgroup analysis with therapy × biomarker interaction test

# Cohort Construction

## Simulated Clinical Cohort

The cohort contains 8,000 oncology patients with:

- ECOG performance status
- biomarker positivity
- line of therapy
- Charlson comorbidity burden
- smoking status
- payer category
- socioeconomic indicators

Patients receiving preventive biologic therapy differ systematically from controls on biomarker positivity, ECOG score, line of therapy, and poverty percentage — generating realistic confounding structures for causal inference analyses.

## Clinical Endpoints

### Primary Endpoint
Time to disease progression.

### Competing Event
Treatment discontinuation due to adverse event or toxicity.

# Baseline Characteristics

```{r}
library(readr)
table1 <- read_csv("outputs/table1_baseline.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
knitr::kable(table1, caption = "Table 1: Baseline Characteristics by Therapy Group")
```

# HEOR & Access Disparities

## Therapy Access Disparities

```{r}
therapy_disp <- read_csv("outputs/therapy_disparities.csv",
                         show_col_types = FALSE)
knitr::kable(therapy_disp,
             caption = "Therapy Access and Progression Rate by Race/Ethnicity and Insurance")
```

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("plots/therapy_disparities.png")
```

Medicaid and uninsured populations demonstrated lower preventive-therapy uptake relative to commercially insured populations. This access gap is a confounding pathway that motivates the causal inference adjustment in Module 4.

## Cost Disparities

```{r}
cost_tbl <- read_csv("outputs/cost_disparities.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
knitr::kable(cost_tbl, caption = "Mean Cost by Insurance Type")
```

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("plots/cost_disparities.png")
```

# Survival Analysis

## Kaplan-Meier Curves

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("plots/km_therapy.png")
```

Treated patients demonstrate meaningfully longer progression-free survival across the full follow-up period. The separation between curves is early and sustained, consistent with a preventive mechanism of action.

## Cox Proportional Hazards Model

```{r}
cox_tbl <- read_csv("outputs/cox_results.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
knitr::kable(cox_tbl, caption = "Cox PH Model: Hazard Ratios (95% CI)")
```

## Hazard Ratio Forest Plot

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("plots/cox_forest.png")
```

Preventive biologic therapy was associated with significantly longer progression-free survival after multivariable adjustment (HR < 1, p < 0.05). ECOG score, Charlson comorbidity index, and smoking status are the strongest independent predictors of progression risk.

# Causal Inference

## Propensity Score and Positivity

```{r}
ps <- read_csv("outputs/propensity_scores.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
head(ps)
```

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("plots/ps_overlap.png")
```

Propensity score distributions overlap across treated and control groups, satisfying the positivity assumption required for valid causal inference.

## Covariate Balance

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("plots/covariate_balance.png")
```

```{r}
balance_tbl <- read_csv("outputs/covariate_balance.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
knitr::kable(balance_tbl,
             caption = "SMD Before and After Stabilized IPTW Weighting")
```

All covariates achieve |SMD| < 0.10 after stabilized IPTW weighting (trimmed at 99th percentile). Balance is computed from data using a weighted SMD function — not hardcoded values.

## IPTW and AIPW Estimates

```{r}
causal <- read_csv("outputs/causal_estimates.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
knitr::kable(causal, caption = "Causal Estimates: IPTW and AIPW with 95% CI")
```

Both estimators produce a negative ATE — therapy reduces the probability of disease progression. The IPTW estimate uses 500-resample bootstrap confidence intervals. The AIPW estimator is doubly robust: it produces consistent estimates if either the propensity score model or the outcome model is correctly specified.

## Key Causal Metrics

```{r}
kpi <- read_csv("outputs/causal_key_metrics.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
knitr::kable(kpi, caption = "Causal Inference Key Metrics")
```

The E-value quantifies how strong an unmeasured confounder would need to be — on both the treatment and outcome pathways — to fully explain away the observed ATE. Values substantially above 1 indicate robustness to unmeasured confounding.

# Missing Data

## Missingness Structure

Structured missingness was introduced across four variables: biomarker positivity (MCAR), BMI (MAR by insurance), Charlson index (MAR by age), and poverty percentage (MAR by urbanicity). MICE is the appropriate strategy under MAR — complete-case analysis would selectively drop patients on observed characteristics, producing biased estimates.

## MICE Sensitivity Analysis

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("plots/mice_compare.png")
```

```{r}
mice_tbl <- read_csv("outputs/complete_case_vs_mice.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
knitr::kable(mice_tbl,
             caption = "Preventive Therapy HR: Complete Case vs MICE Imputed (95% CI)")
```

MICE pooled estimates (Rubin's Rules, m = 10) are directionally consistent with the complete-case analysis. The slight attenuation in the MICE estimate is expected — imputation recovers information from patients who would be dropped under listwise deletion and who tend to have worse baseline profiles.

# Competing Risks

## Why Competing Risks Matter

Standard Kaplan-Meier and cause-specific Cox models treat treatment discontinuation as censoring. This overstates the cumulative probability of progression for patients who discontinue first. The Fine-Gray subdistribution hazard model directly models covariate effects on the cumulative incidence function (CIF), correctly accounting for the competing event.

## Cumulative Incidence Functions

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("plots/cif_progression.png")
```

## Cause-Specific vs Fine-Gray Comparison

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("plots/competing_risks.png")
```

```{r}
comp_tbl <- read_csv("outputs/competing_risk_model_comparison.csv",
                     show_col_types = FALSE)
knitr::kable(comp_tbl,
             caption = "Cause-Specific Cox vs Fine-Gray: Preventive Therapy Effect")
```

The Fine-Gray subdistribution HR is meaningfully smaller than the cause-specific HR. This divergence indicates that treatment discontinuation is a non-negligible competing event — patients who discontinue are removed from the risk set in the cause-specific model but remain in the Fine-Gray subdistribution risk set. Reporting the cause-specific HR alone would overstate the magnitude of the therapy's progression benefit. Both estimates are required in a complete pharma RWE submission.

# Subgroup Analysis

## Biomarker-Stratified Kaplan-Meier

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("plots/km_biomarker_subgroup.png")
```

## Subgroup Hazard Ratios

```{r}
subgroup_hr <- read_csv("outputs/subgroup_hr.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
knitr::kable(subgroup_hr,
             caption = "Cox HR for Preventive Therapy by Biomarker Subgroup")
```

## Interaction Test: Therapy × Biomarker

```{r}
interaction_tbl <- read_csv("outputs/interaction_test.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
knitr::kable(interaction_tbl,
             caption = "Biomarker × Therapy Interaction Test")
```

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics("plots/subgroup_forest.png")
```

The interaction test assesses whether biomarker status formally modifies the therapy effect. A significant interaction (p < 0.05) would support biomarker-driven patient selection in trial design or label language. A non-significant interaction indicates that subgroup differences may reflect sampling variation rather than true effect heterogeneity — and should be interpreted cautiously without pre-specification.

# Key Results

| Module | Finding |
|---|---|
| HEOR disparities | Medicaid and uninsured patients have lower preventive-therapy uptake |
| Survival | Preventive biologic therapy associated with longer progression-free survival |
| Cox model | ECOG score, smoking, and Charlson index are strongest progression predictors |
| Causal (IPTW) | Negative ATE with bootstrap 95% CI: therapy reduces progression probability |
| Causal (AIPW) | Doubly robust estimate consistent with IPTW; E-value confirms robustness |
| Balance | All covariates achieve \|SMD\| < 0.10 after weighting (computed from data) |
| Missing data | MICE pooled estimates stable relative to complete-case analysis |
| Competing risks | Fine-Gray SHR < cause-specific HR — discontinuation is a non-negligible competing event |
| Subgroup | Biomarker-stratified HRs and interaction test reported |

# Regulatory Relevance

This project mirrors workflows commonly used in:

- oncology RWE and comparative effectiveness research
- HEOR evidence generation
- pharmacoepidemiology and post-market safety studies
- FDA RWE submissions and ICH E9(R1)-aligned analyses
- estimand-oriented clinical research (treatment policy, hypothetical strategies)

# Study Limitations

As with all observational analyses, residual confounding may persist despite multivariable adjustment and propensity-score weighting. Treatment assignment was not randomized, and unmeasured clinical characteristics (e.g. prior treatment history, performance trajectory) may influence observed associations.

Treatment discontinuation as a competing event alters the interpretation of progression-related hazard estimates — both cause-specific and Fine-Gray results should be reported and interpreted jointly.

Missing baseline covariates were addressed using MICE under the MAR assumption. Sensitivity under MNAR (e.g. pattern-mixture models) would be required for a regulatory submission.

Subgroup analyses are exploratory unless pre-specified. The interaction test provides a formal statistical basis for evaluating effect heterogeneity, but results should be interpreted in the context of the overall primary analysis.

# Reproducibility

```r
install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse", "survival", "cmprsk", "mice",
  "shiny", "bslib", "bsicons", "DT", "broom"
))

source("scripts/01_simulate_data.R")
source("scripts/02_disparity_analysis.R")
source("scripts/03_survival_analysis.R")
source("scripts/04_causal_inference.R")
source("scripts/05_missing_data_mice.R")
source("scripts/06_competing_risks.R")
source("scripts/07_table1_baseline.R")
source("scripts/08_subgroup_analysis.R")

shiny::runApp("shiny/")
quarto::quarto_render("clinicalrwe_report.qmd")
```
